name: performance
context: Make pandas workflows fit memory and run faster with profiling, efficient dtypes, vectorization, chunking, Parquet, Arrow, and handoffs
info: Pandas runs in one Python process and is constrained by memory, dtype choices, and whether operations are vectorized. Agents use this node when a dataset is slow, too large, object-heavy, or repeatedly read from CSV. It teaches profiling with `memory_usage(deep=True)`, selecting columns early, converting repeated strings to categorical values, using nullable and Arrow-backed dtypes, chunking CSV reductions, writing Parquet artifacts, avoiding row loops, and handing work to [[duckdb]] when SQL over large files is the better execution model.
triggers: read a large csv with pandas
when to use duckdb instead of pandas
body:
# Pandas Performance

Optimize by reducing data read, reducing memory per column, avoiding Python loops, and switching engines when pandas is the wrong tool.

## Measure

```python
print(df.shape)
print(df.memory_usage(deep=True).sort_values(ascending=False).head(20))
print(df.dtypes.value_counts())
```

## Reduce memory

```python
df = pd.read_csv('events.csv', usecols=['user_id', 'event', 'ts'])
df['event'] = df['event'].astype('category')
df['user_id'] = df['user_id'].astype('string')
df.to_parquet('events.parquet', index=False)
```

## Chunk CSV reductions

```python
totals = []
for chunk in pd.read_csv('orders.csv', chunksize=100_000, usecols=['status', 'amount']):
    chunk['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(chunk['amount'], errors='coerce')
    totals.append(chunk.groupby('status')['amount'].sum())
summary = pd.concat(totals, axis=1).sum(axis=1).reset_index(name='amount')
```

## Handoff

Use [[duckdb]] for SQL joins and aggregations over large CSV or Parquet files, especially when the operation can be expressed as a query and does not need complex Python functions.

sections.title: Profile memory
sections.body: Use `df.memory_usage(deep=True)` to see true memory use for object columns. The shared `profile_dataset.py` script reports total memory, dtypes, and top values. Large object columns with repeated text are prime candidates for `category` or Arrow-backed strings.

sections.title: Read less data
sections.body: Use `usecols`, Parquet `columns`, row filters before joins, and sampling with `nrows` to avoid reading unused data. Convert repeated CSV reads into Parquet artifacts. Drop unneeded columns before high-cardinality joins or groupby operations.

sections.title: Efficient dtypes
sections.body: Use `category` for low-cardinality strings, nullable integer and boolean dtypes when missing values exist, and Arrow-backed dtypes when interoperating with Arrow/Parquet-heavy workflows. Explicit dtypes reduce memory and prevent accidental object columns.

sections.title: Vectorize or hand off
sections.body: Prefer vectorized pandas operations, built-in reductions, and string/datetime accessors. Avoid `iterrows` and row-wise `apply` for large data. If the task is SQL-shaped over large files, use [[duckdb]]. If it needs distributed compute, pandas is not the right engine.
